root_text.sn47.21:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ āyasmā ca ānando āyasmā ca bhaddo pāṭaliputte viharanti kukkuṭārāme.
root_text.sn47.21:1.3: Atha kho āyasmā bhaddo sāyanhasamayaṁ paṭisallānā vuṭṭhito yenāyasmā ānando tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā āyasmatā ānandena saddhiṁ sammodi.
root_text.sn47.21:1.4: Sammodanīyaṁ kathaṁ sāraṇīyaṁ vītisāretvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho āyasmā bhaddo āyasmantaṁ ānandaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn47.21:1.5: “yānimāni, āvuso ānanda, kusalāni sīlāni vuttāni bhagavatā, imāni kusalāni sīlāni kimatthiyāni vuttāni bhagavatā”ti?
root_text.sn47.21:2.2: Bhaddako kho te, āvuso bhadda, ummaṅgo, bhaddakaṁ paṭibhānaṁ, kalyāṇī paripucchā.
root_text.sn47.21:2.4: ‘yānimāni, āvuso ānanda, kusalāni sīlāni vuttāni bhagavatā, imāni kusalāni sīlāni kimatthiyāni vuttāni bhagavatā’”ti?
root_text.sn47.21:2.6: “Yānimāni, āvuso bhadda, kusalāni sīlāni vuttāni bhagavatā, imāni kusalāni sīlāni yāvadeva catunnaṁ satipaṭṭhānānaṁ bhāvanāya vuttāni bhagavatā.
root_text.sn47.21:3.2: Idhāvuso, bhikkhu kāye kāyānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;
root_text.sn47.21:3.5: dhammesu dhammānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ.
root_text.sn47.21:3.6: Yānimāni, āvuso bhadda, kusalāni sīlāni vuttāni bhagavatā, imāni kusalāni sīlāni yāvadeva imesaṁ catunnaṁ satipaṭṭhānānaṁ bhāvanāya vuttāni bhagavatā”ti.

translation_text.sn47.21:1.2: At one time the venerables Ānanda and Bhadda were staying near Pāṭaliputta, in the Chicken Monastery.
translation_text.sn47.21:1.3: Then in the late afternoon, Venerable Bhadda came out of retreat, went to Venerable Ānanda, and exchanged greetings with him.
translation_text.sn47.21:1.4: When the greetings and polite conversation were over, he sat down to one side and said to Ānanda:
translation_text.sn47.21:1.5: “Reverend Ānanda, the Buddha has spoken of skillful ethics. What’s their purpose?”
translation_text.sn47.21:2.2: Your approach and articulation are excellent, and it’s a good question.
translation_text.sn47.21:2.4: ‘The Buddha has spoken of skillful ethics. What’s their purpose?’”
translation_text.sn47.21:2.6: “The Buddha has spoken of skillful ethics to the extent necessary for developing the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.
translation_text.sn47.21:3.2: It’s when a mendicant meditates by observing an aspect of the body—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.21:3.3: They meditate observing an aspect of feelings …
translation_text.sn47.21:3.5: principles—keen, aware, and mindful, rid of covetousness and displeasure for the world.
translation_text.sn47.21:3.6: The Buddha has spoken of skillful ethics to the extent necessary for developing the four kinds of mindfulness meditation.”
